section.p: In this step, we'll estimate your and your spouse's joint earned income, adjusted gross income, and any adjustments to it.
Please select and enter your and your spouse's income and adjustments. Only select one item per source. All fields marked with an asterisk are required.

div.p: Please note: Make sure to also include your spouse's income and adjustments.

fg-set.question: Wages, salary, or tips where federal income taxes are withheld
fg-set.modal-link: What income should I include?

fg-set.question: Self-employment Gross income
fg-set.modal-link: What is self-employment income?

fg-set.question: Total self-employment work-related expenses you and your spouse deducted in
fg-set.hint: It is okay to leave this blank.
fg-set.modal-link: What are self-employment deductible expenses?

fg-set.question: Disability retirement benefits before minimum retirement age
fg-set.modal-link: What are disability retirement benefits?

fg-set.question: Foreign earned income not reported on Form 2555
fg-set.modal-link: What is foreign earned income?

fg-set.question: Investment income
fg-set.modal-link: What is your total investment income?

fg-set.question: Taxable portion of any pension
fg-set.modal-link: What is the taxable portion of a pension?

fg-set.question: Unemployment insurance
fg-set.modal-link: What counts as unemployment insurance?

fg-detail.summary: Social Security and railroad retirement benefits

fg-set.question: Taxable portion of Social Security and Railroad Retirement Benefits
fg-set.modal-link: What counts as Social Security and Railroad Retirement Benefits?

fg-set.question: A scholarship or grant that must be counted as income
fg-set.modal-link: What counts as a scholarship or grant?

fg-set.question: Pay received for work while an inmate in a penal institution
fg-set.modal-link: What is inmate pay?

fg-set.question: Total amount of any other income you and your spouse received in
fg-set.modal-link: What are other taxable incomes?
fg-set.hint: You should enter one total amount for all other income.

section.p: Select and enter any of these types of adjustments to income you had in . Do not enter the same adjustment in multiple places.

fg-set.question: Student loan interest you you and your spouse paid in
fg-set.modal-link: What is the student loan interest deduction?

fg-set.question: Educator expenses youyou and your spouse paid in
fg-set.modal-link: What are educator expenses?

fg-detail.summary: Deduction for contributions to an IRA that is not included in payroll deductions

fg-set.question: Traditional (pre-tax) IRA contributions youyou and your spouse made in
fg-set.modal-link: What counts as traditional IRA contributions?

fg-set.question: Self-employed retirement contributions youyou and your spouse made in
fg-set.modal-link: What is the self-employed retirement plan deduction?

fg-set.question: Health insurance premiums youyou and your spouse paid in
fg-set.modal-link: What is the self-employed health insurance deduction?

fg-detail.summary: Health Savings Account (HSA) deductions that are not included in payroll deductions

fg-set.question: HSA contributions youyou or your spouse made in
fg-set.modal-link: What are HSA deductions?

fg-set.question: Alimony you paid in
fg-set.modal-link: What counts as alimony paid?

fg-detail.summary: Penalty for early withdrawal of savings

fg-set.question: Penalty amount youyou and your spouse paid in
fg-set.modal-link: What is the penalty for early withdrawal of savings?

fg-detail.summary: Moving expenses for members of the armed forces

fg-set.question: Moving expenses for members of the armed forces youyou and your spouse paid in
fg-set.modal-link: What counts as moving expenses for the armed forces?

fg-set.question: Eligible business expenses youyou and your spouse paid in
fg-set.modal-link: What are eligible employee business expenses?

fg-set.question: Other adjustments youyou and your spouse paid in

fg-alert.heading: You don't qualify for the Earned Income Tax Credit (EITC)
fg-alert.p: Based on your adjusted gross income (including the adjustments you entered), you don’t qualify for the EITC for .
For more information, see Publication 596, Earned Income Credit.

modal-content.p: Your employer reported your wages, salaries, tips, and other compensation in box 1 of your Form W-2.
Include the full amount of tip and overtime income in the calculation of earned income when determining eligibility for the earned income tax credit, even if all or part is deductible.
Include all amounts reported in box 1 of Form W-2 for both you and your spouse if you're filing a joint tax return with your spouse.
Don't include your Form W-2 pre-tax contributions in the pre-tax contributions section.
You can also choose to include your nontaxable combat pay in earned income for the EITC. Your employer reported your nontaxable combat pay in box 12 with code Q on your Form W-2.

This choice may increase or decrease your EITC. Calculate your EITC both ways to see which option provides the greater benefit. For more information, see Nontaxable combat pay in Publication 596, Earned Income Credit.
If you took parental leave or were temporarily disabled during the year and your employer paid you, they reported your income on your W-2. Enter that income here.

modal-dialog.modal-heading: Income Where Federal Taxes Weren't Withheld

modal-content.p: Report income earned from gig work, even if it's a side job, part time or temporary.
Enter income here if you earn money from a job even if income taxes were not withheld. You may or may not have received a Form 1099 for all income.
The income may have been earned through an app or website to:

ul.li: Drive a car for booked rides or deliveries
Provide other temporary, on-demand or freelance work

modal-content.p: Your gross income includes all the income you received. We will subtract your expenses later.
For more information, visit Manage Taxes for Your Gig Work

ul.li: Are self-employed (You may or may not receive a Form 1099 for all income)
Are a minister or member of a religious order

modal-content.p: Your gross income includes all the income you received. We will subtract your expenses later.
For more information, visit Self-employed individuals tax center

If you retired on disability, we consider the taxable benefits you received under your employer's disability retirement plan earned income for the purposes of the EITC until you reach minimum retirement age.
The minimum retirement age is the earliest age at which you could have received a pension or annuity if you weren't disabled.
Include temporary or short-term disability in your wages.
Pay you received for work you performed while an inmate in a penal institution isn't earned income when figuring the earned income credit.
This includes paid work performed while in a work release program or while in a halfway house.
For more information, see Publication 596, Earned Income Credit, Earnings while an inmate.
Foreign earned income is income earned while living and working outside the United States, District of Columbia, or U.S. possessions or territories.
If you’re a U.S. government employee working outside the United States, that income is not considered foreign earned.
If you're not a government employee and you work at an embassy, we consider that income as foreign income for the EITC.
For more information, see Form 2555, Foreign Earned Income.
Note: If you elect to file Form 2555, you are not eligible to claim the EITC.
If you receive retirement benefits in the form of pension or annuity payments from a qualified employer retirement plan, all or some portion of the amounts you receive may be taxable.

ul.li: You didn't contribute anything or aren't considered to have contributed anything for your pension or annuity
Your employer didn't withhold contributions from your salary, or
You received all your contributions tax-free in prior years
You contributed after-tax dollars (use the General Rule and Simplified Method to calculate)

modal-content.p: Additional rules apply if you received payments before age 59 and a half, are a survivor or beneficiary, or have other situations. For more information visit Tax Topic 410.
For the EITC, include your full unemployment compensation benefits. You find this income on Form 1099-G.
If your employer's fund provided supplemental benefits, include any amounts reported on Form W-2, box 1.

modal-dialog.modal-heading: Social Security and Railroad Retirement Benefits

modal-content.p: For the EITC, include only the taxable amount of your Social Security and railroad retirement benefits.
IRS Notice 703 can help you determine if any of your Social Security benefits are taxable. For more information visit Tax Topic 423.
To compute the taxable amount of your benefits, see "Which worksheet to use" in Publication 915, Social Security and Equivalent Railroad Retirement Benefits.
Enter any amount of taxable scholarship not reported on your Form W-2.
This amount includes scholarship or grant money not found on your W-2 if the money was:

ul.li: Used for incidental expenses, such as room and board, travel, and optional equipment
Received as payment for teaching, research, or other services required as a condition for receiving the scholarship or fellowship grant

modal-content.p: Don't include amounts you received for services required by the National Health Service Corps Scholarship Program, the Armed Forces Health Professions Scholarship and Financial Assistance Program, or a comprehensive student work-learning-service program operated by a work college.
For more information visit Tax Topic 421.

fg-alert.heading: You don’t qualify for the Earned Income Tax Credit
fg-alert.p: Based on the information you provided, you can’t claim the earned income credit because your earned income is over the limit for your filing status and number of qualifying children.
For more information, see Publication 596, Earned Income Credit.

fg-alert.heading: You don’t qualify for the Earned Income Tax Credit (EITC)
fg-alert.p: To claim the EITC, you must have had earned income (for example, wages or net earnings from self-employment). Based on the income you entered, your earned income for was zero or less, so you don’t qualify for the EITC.
For more information, see Publication 596, Earned Income Credit.

fg-alert.heading: You don’t qualify for the Earned Income Tax Credit (EITC)

div.p: You don't qualify for the Earned Income Tax Credit (EITC) if your investment income was more than .
For more information, see Publication 550, Investment Income and Expenses. To figure your total investment income, you should use Worksheet 1 in Publication 596, Earned Income Credit.

modal-content.p: When you were self employed, a gig worker, or owned and operated a business, you may have been able to deduct certain business expenses from your income so you paid less tax.
Business expenses can include:
These deductions are different from the personal deductions you made if you chose to itemize your deductions. You can deduct your business expenses here and still choose the standard deduction later.

p.strong: How to check last year's tax return?

modal-content.p: If you were self-employed in you can find your deductible expenses on line 28 of Schedule C.

modal-dialog.modal-heading: What was interest income and how can I calculate it?

modal-content.p: Common types of interest income include interest earned from bank accounts or from lending money.

p.strong: How to check last year's tax return

modal-content.p: If you earned interest income in , check for an amount in:

ul.li: Line 2b on Form 1040 of your federal tax return

modal-content.p: Investment income for the EITC includes:
See Publication 550, Investment Income and Expenses for more information.
Use Worksheet 1 in Publication 596, Earned Income Credit, to figure your investment income if any of the following statements are true for 2025:

ul.li: You are reporting income from the rental of personal property not used in a trade or business.
You are reporting from Form 8814 (relating to election to report child's interest and dividends).
You have income or loss from a passive activity.
You are filing Form 4797 (relating to sales of business property).

modal-dialog.modal-content:
'Other types of income include:'
Individual Retirement Account (IRA) distributions (except qualified distributions from Roth IRAs) Trust and 401k distributions
Capital losses (not gains)
Alimony payments you received under a divorce or separation agreement executed before 2020
Rental real estate, royalties, partnerships, S corporations, trusts, etc.
Income from rental of personal property if you engaged in the rental for profit but were not in the business of renting such property
Taxable distributions from a Coverdell education savings account (ESA) and qualified tuition program earnings.
Qualified tuition program earnings
Prizes, awards, and gambling winnings
Jury duty fees
Alaska Permanent Fund dividends
Reimbursements or other amounts received for items deducted in an earlier year, such as medical expenses, real estate taxes, or home mortgage interest
'Do not include:,'
Alimony payments you received under a divorce or separation agreement entered into after 2019
Child support
Welfare benefits such as Temporary Assistance for Needy Families (TANF), food stamps, section 8 housing allowance
Nontaxable foster care payments
Nontaxable employee benefits such as education assistance
Veterans' benefits, including VA rehabilitation payments
Workman's compensation
Life insurance proceeds received because of a person's death
Money or property that was inherited, willed to you, or received as a gift
Unemployment benefits
See Publication 525, Taxable and Nontaxable Income

modal-content.p: Enter up to of the interest you paid on your qualified student loan. You can find this on your Form 1098-E if you paid more than $600 in interest.
The loan must have paid qualified higher education expenses to attend an eligible educational institution. Also, the expenses must have been:

ul.li: For you, your spouse, or a person who was your dependent when you took out the loan;
Paid or incurred within a reasonable period of time before or after you took out the loan; and
For education provided during an academic period for an eligible student.

modal-content.p: You can't claim the deduction if you aren't legally obligated to make payments on the loan.
For more information, see Publication 970, Tax Benefits for Education (opens in a new tab).
If you're a teacher, enter up to ( if married filing jointly and both you and your spouse are teachers) of unreimbursed expenses such as:

ul.li: Fees for participation in professional development courses
Computer equipment (including related software and services)
Supplementary materials that you use in the classroom

modal-content.p: For more information, see IRS Topic 458 (opens in a new tab).

modal-dialog.modal-heading: Deduction for contributions to an IRA

modal-content.p: Enter any IRA contributions you made outside of your payroll contributions.
For more information see, IRA Deduction Limits (opens in a new tab).
If you or your spouse were self employed and made contributions to qualified retirement plans in , you may have been eligible for the self-employed retirement plan deduction.
To qualify, you or your spouse must have:

ul.li: Been a working partner in a partnership, or

modal-content.p: Eligible plans may have included:

ul.li: Savings Incentive Match Plan for Employees

modal-content.p: Don't include Individual Retirement Arrangement (IRA) contributions.
You must have used a special rule to calculate retirement plan contributions for yourself.

p.a: Learn more about self-employed retirement contributions. (opens in a new tab)

modal-content.p: Enter any Health Savings Account (HSA) contributions that you didn't include under HSA, FSA, or other “cafeteria plan” above.
For more information visit About Publication 969, Health Savings Accounts and Other Tax-Favored Health Plans (opens in a new tab).

If you made alimony or separate maintenance payments under a court order that was executed before January 1, 2019 and the order didn't specifically state that you can't deduct your payments, enter the amount you paid during year here.
Don't include amounts you paid for:
Also don't include amounts if the court order was modified and states that you can't deduct your payments.
Alimony or separate maintenance made under a court order executed after December 31, 2024
For more information, see Tax Topic 452 (opens in a new tab).

modal-dialog.modal-heading: Penalty for early withdrawal of savings

modal-content.p: If you withdraw funds from a certificate of deposit or other deferred interest account before maturity, you may be charged a penalty. You can find this penalty on your Form 1099-INT in box 2. Enter that amount here.

modal-dialog.modal-heading: Moving expenses for members of the armed forces

modal-content.p: If you are a member of the armed forces on active duty, and the military ordered you to permanently change your duty station, enter any moving expenses the military didn't reimburse you for here. Moving expenses include:

ul.li: Direct travel from your old home to your new one
Cost of packing and moving your personal belongings

modal-content.p: For more information, see Tax Topic 455 (opens in a new tab).
If you are a/an:

ul.li: Fee-basis state or local government official

modal-content.p: You may include expenses that are common and accepted in your field of trade, business, or profession, or expenses for things that are helpful and appropriate for your business.
If you don't fit into one of the listed categories, you may not claim adjustments for these expenses.
For more information and a list of qualified expenses, see Form 2106, Employee Business Expenses (opens in a new tab).
If you or your spouse were self employed and had a net profit for the year, you could have deducted your health insurance premiums.

Eligible premiums were medical, dental, vision, and long-term care insurance. The policy could be for yourself, your spouse, and your dependents. The policy could also cover your child who was under the age of 27 at the end of the year, even if the child wasn't your dependent.

p.a: Learn more about self-employed health insurance. (opens in a new tab)
